title: Type, class, attribute and combinator selectors

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Type, class, attribute and combinator selectors; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Type, class, attribute and combinator selectors
sources.claim: Selectors match elements by type, class, id, attribute, and relationship, and combinators express ancestry or sibling structure.

sources.locator: MDN CSS Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Type, class, attribute and combinator selectors
sources.claim: Selectors match elements by type, class, id, attribute, and relationship, and combinators express ancestry or sibling structure.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Selectors match elements by type, class, id, attribute, and relationship, and combinators express ancestry or sibling structure.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A .card__title class targets the component role without depending on three wrapper levels. Your goal is to choose selectors that are specific enough without coupling to incidental nesting. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Specificity and source order; html semantics; Rules, declarations and values; html document dom. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Selectors match elements by type, class, id, attribute, and relationship, and combinators express ancestry or sibling structure. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Overly structural selectors break when markup changes, ids create high specificity, and selector matching does not alter DOM structure. This draft therefore rejects: A selector describes how the browser should create or rearrange HTML elements.

includes: Select elements by stable semantic hooks and structural relationships.
Choose selectors that are specific enough without coupling to incidental nesting.
excludes: Overly structural selectors break when markup changes, ids create high specificity, and selector matching does not alter DOM structure.

outcomes: Choose selectors that are specific enough without coupling to incidental nesting.

examples.title: Type, class, attribute and combinator selectors: concrete case
examples.situation: A .card__title class targets the component role without depending on three wrapper levels.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A .card__title class targets the component role without depending on three wrapper levels.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Selectors match existing elements; use stable semantic classes and relationships without coupling to unnecessary wrapper depth.
examples.result: The result follows because Selectors match existing elements; use stable semantic classes and relationships without coupling to unnecessary wrapper depth.

Step 3 — Reject the misconception: A selector describes how the browser should create or rearrange HTML elements.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Type, class, attribute and combinator selectors, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Type, class, attribute and combinator selectors before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.
